competitors.label: MDN this
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference competitor for every serious `this` explanation, especially on edge cases and invocation forms.
theirStrengths: It is the most complete reference for call-site rules across functions, methods, constructors, modules, and arrows.
theirGaps: It is reference-first rather than compactly optimized for debugging detached-method failures.

realWorldUseCases: MDN already covers callbacks, methods, constructors, and module behavior, so the real-world edge is close rather than decisive.
actionableExamples: MDN has many concrete invocation examples, so the worked-example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: MDN answers more long-tail follow-ups such as top-level module `this`, class fields, and multiple invocation forms.

realWorldUseCases: Add one explicit detached-method production bug so the debug framing is stronger than a general reference page.
actionableExamples: Add one side-by-side example of the same function succeeding and failing under two different call sites.
followUpConfusion: Add one concise note about top-level module `this` and class-field arrow methods.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd this binding
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant interview-prep explanation of `this`, so it is required for calibration against interview-oriented competitors.
theirStrengths: It is aligned with the same interview question and covers the core mental model with direct examples.
theirGaps: It is less explicitly bug-first than our detached-method framing.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd already frames `this` as an interview topic with realistic invocation scenarios, so the practical angle is close.
actionableExamples: Its quiz-style examples make the worked-example criterion competitive.
followUpConfusion: It covers the binding rules broadly enough that it currently handles more follow-up questions than our shorter page.

realWorldUseCases: Keep the callback-context-loss framing, but make one browser or framework callback scenario more explicit.
actionableExamples: Add one single code sample that contrasts plain call, method call, and bound call in one place.
followUpConfusion: Add one tighter summary of the uncommon but common-in-interviews cases: modules, class fields, and extracted methods.

competitors.label: DigitalOcean this / bind / call / apply
competitors.selectionReason: This tutorial is a common reference competitor for practical binding fixes like bind, call, and apply.
competitors.winReason: Our page is more concise on the receiver-at-call-time mental model and keeps the detached-method debugging angle tighter than the broader DigitalOcean tutorial.
theirStrengths: It explains bind, call, and apply with approachable examples and common browser contexts.
theirGaps: It is broader and less compact on the core receiver-at-call-time mental model.

realWorldUseCases: The real debug problem is detached methods, callback context loss, and object methods that stop pointing at the receiver you expected.
followUpConfusion: So the useful mental model is not 'owner of the function' but 'receiver at call time.'

realWorldUseCases: DigitalOcean covers practical contexts, but it is less centered on the detached-method bug pattern our page foregrounds.
actionableExamples: Its bind, call, and apply examples are solid enough that the example criterion remains close.
followUpConfusion: It teaches explicit binding well, but the core debugging heuristic is spread across a longer walkthrough.

actionableExamples: Add one compact example involving method extraction and delayed invocation so the tutorial-reference comparison is easier to win.
